content: Identify the element having highest value of first ionization enthalpy.
explanation:
The ionization enthalpy decreases down the group due to increase in the atomic size.

Therefore, ionization enthalpy of Ar is more than that of Xe.

Across a period ionization enthalpy increases with increase of atomic number.

Therefore, ionization enthalpy of Se and Br is in the order: $\mathrm{Se}<\mathrm{Br}$.

Hence, the correct increasing order of ionization enthalpy for the given elements is:

$$ \mathrm{Se}<\mathrm{Br}<\mathrm{Xe}<\mathrm{Ar} . $$

content:
If four different elements $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}$ and D having outer electronic configuration as,

$$ \mathrm{A}=3 \mathrm{~s}^2 3 \mathrm{p}^4, \mathrm{~B}=3 \mathrm{~s}^2 3 \mathrm{p}^5, \mathrm{C}=4 \mathrm{~s}^2 4 \mathrm{p}^4, \mathrm{D}=4 \mathrm{~s}^2 4 \mathrm{p}^5 $$

identify the element with lowest ionization enthalpy $\left(\Delta_1 \mathrm{H}_1\right)$
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{|l|l|l|} \hline & \text { Group 16 } & \text { Group 17 } \\ \hline \text { Period 3 } & \mathrm{A}\left(3 \mathrm{~s}^2 3 \mathrm{p}^4\right) & \mathrm{B}\left(3 \mathrm{~s}^2 3 \mathrm{p}^5\right) \\ \hline \text { Period 4 } & \mathrm{C}\left(4 \mathrm{~s}^2 4 \mathrm{p}^4\right) & \mathrm{D}\left(4 \mathrm{~s}^2 4 \mathrm{p}^5\right) \\ \hline \end{array} $$

On moving along the period, effective nuclear charge increases, hence ionization enthalpy also increases. As we go down the group, the effective nuclear charge decreases. Hence the first ionization enthalpy decreases down the group. Therefore element C has the lowest ionization enthalpy.

content:
If $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}, \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{D}$ are four different elements with outer electronic configuration as $\mathrm{A}=4 \mathrm{~s}^2 4 \mathrm{p}^4, \mathrm{~B}=4 \mathrm{~s}^2 4 \mathrm{p}^5, \mathrm{C}=5 \mathrm{~s}^2 5 \mathrm{p}^4, \mathrm{D}=5 \mathrm{~s}^2 5 \mathrm{p}^5$.

Find the element having highest ionization enthalpy $\left(\Delta_1 \mathrm{H}_1\right)$
explanation:
Order of ionization enthalpy (IE) is governed by two main trends:

Across a period (left to right): IE increases (nuclear charge ↑, similar shielding).

Down a group: IE decreases (atomic size ↑, valence electron farther, more shielding).

Elements given:

A: $4s^2\,4p^4$ (Se, period 4, group 16)

B: $4s^2\,4p^5$ (Br, period 4, group 17)

C: $5s^2\,5p^4$ (Te, period 5, group 16)

D: $5s^2\,5p^5$ (I, period 5, group 17)

Compare:

• Between A and B (same period 4): B has one extra proton and one more electron in the same shell ⇒ IE_B > IE_A.

• Between C and D (same period 5): IE_D > IE_C.

• Between periods 4 and 5 (same group): IE (period 4) > IE (period 5).

So ranking highest to lowest IE:

B (Br) > A (Se) > D (I) > C (Te).

Therefore, the element with the highest first ionization enthalpy is B.
